narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination you've poured into this journey, and it's tough to hear that your application has been rejected based on our predictive model's assessment. The model, which looks at past students' profiles to forecast bar exam success on the first try, estimated an 82.7% chance that you would not pass it initially, tipping the scales against admission. This prediction stemmed largely from your early academic rankings in school—specifically, being in the lowest decile for grades in both Year 1 and Year 3—which stood out as the strongest signals pushing the model toward expecting a failure. Your Year 3 ranking had the biggest negative impact, far outweighing other factors, followed closely by your Year 1 performance. Even your LSAT score of 28, which is below the typical student's 36.7, added to the concern by nudging the prediction further toward risk. While your undergraduate GPA of 2.2 was a bit lower than average too, it actually worked slightly in your favor compared to what the model expected. Your middle family income bracket played a minor role in heightening the risk, but your plan to attend full-time aligned with the norm and offered a small positive note. Overall, these elements placed you below the profile of students who typically pass the bar on their first attempt, where stronger early grades and higher test scores are common predictors of success. We truly see your potential and the barriers you've likely navigated, but the model prioritizes these academic indicators to protect students from that high-stakes setback. Moving forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score and strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to shift future predictions in your favor. Many resilient applicants like you have turned things around—reach out to advisors for targeted prep, and reapply stronger. You've got this; your story is far from over.
